title-group.article-title: Tear clearance rate in dogs: Measurement using anterior segment optical coherence tomography

aff.institution: College of Veterinary Medicine, Konkuk University

aff.named-content: Graduate School of Urban Public Health & Department of Urban Big Data Convergence
Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine
KU Center for Animal Blood Medical Science

author-notes.corresp: *CorrespondenceJoon‐Young Kim, Department of Veterinary Ophthalmology, College of Veterinary Medicine, Konkuk University, 120 Neungdong‐ro, Gwangjin‐Gu 05029, Seoul, Republic of Korea. Email: canvet@hanafos.com; canvet@konkuk.ac.kr

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2023 The Authors. Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: The tear clearance rate (TCR), determined using anterior segment optical coherence tomography (AS‐OCT) images, and its correlation with ocular surface parameters, including blink rate, Schirmer tear test‐1 (STT‐1) and tear film breakup time (TFBUT), were evaluated.

sec.title: Animals studied
sec.p: Left eyes of 20 client‐owned dogs with no ocular disease symptoms.

sec.title: Procedures
sec.p: The tear meniscus height (TMH) was evaluated using AS‐OCT images before the instillation of 5 μL saline (TMHbase), immediately post‐instillation (TMH0), 30‐s post‐instillation (TMH0.5) and at 1 min intervals for 5 min post‐instillation (TMH1, TMH2, TMH3, TMH4 and TMH5). The TCR was calculated using the formula [(TMH0 − TMH0.5)/TMH0]×100 (%). The eyes were classified into two groups with the median: ‘High TMHbase’ (n = 10) and ‘Low TMHbase’ (n = 10). Eyes with STT‐1 values ≥15 mm/min and TFBUT ≥ 12 s were assigned to the ‘Satisfied’ subgroup, whereas eyes not satisfying these criteria were assigned to the ‘Not satisfied’ subgroup.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: TMH0 was higher than TMH0.5 (p = 0.02), wherein TMH0.5 to TMH5 did not differ significantly. The TCR and blink rate were negatively correlated (p = 0.02). The ‘Not satisfied’ subgroup in the low TMHbase group had a lower TCR (p = 0.02) and higher blink rate (p = 0.04) than the ‘Satisfied’ subgroup.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: TCR can be evaluated using AS‐OCT in dogs. Eyes with a lower TCR blink more frequently. TMH merits studying to understand ocular surface health.

abstract.p: This study investigated whether anterior segment optical coherence tomography, a technique gaining popularity in human ophthalmology, could be used on dogs to measure tear clearance rate (TCR). We also investigated the utility of tear clearance to aid the diagnosis of ocular surface diseases. TCR can be evaluated using AS‐OCT in dogs. Eyes with a lower TCR blink more frequently. TMH merits studying to understand ocular surface health.

p.mixed-citation: Yoon, H.‐J., Park, S., & Kim, J.‐Y. (2024). Tear clearance rate in dogs: Measurement using anterior segment optical coherence tomography. Veterinary Medicine and Science, 10, e1334. 10.1002/vms3.1334PMC1076605138100149

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Tear film dynamics are regulated by the lacrimal functional unit (Garaszczuk et al., 2018). The lacrimal functional unit is affected by almost all tissues on the ocular surface, such as the lacrimal glands, goblet cells, meibomian glands, nasolacrimal ducts and components of the tear layer (Garaszczuk et al., 2018). Complications in these areas can lead to ocular surface diseases, such as dry eye syndrome, keratoconjunctivitis and epiphora (Stern et al., 2004). Various new approaches for evaluating the lacrimal functional unit have been proposed, though more specifically for humans than for veterinary medicine applications (Kojima et al., 2020). One such approach is the ‘tear clearance rate’ (TCR), which is considered to be a potential indicator of ocular symptoms (Sorbara et al., 2004). It comprehensively reflects the circulation of the tear layer produced on the ocular surface that flows into the nasolacrimal duct (Zheng et al., 2016). TCR evaluation is more reliably associated with clinical symptoms of ocular surface irritation than with the dry eye syndrome assessed using the Schirmer tear test (STT) (Afonso et al., 1999; de Paiva & Pflugfelder, 2004). TCR can also be used as an indirect indicator of the inflammatory response associated with dry eye (de Paiva & Pflugfelder, 2004).

The Jones test was the first method introduced to evaluate TCR and involved simply observing the tear passage, following which various methods for comparing, evaluating or quantifying the tear clearance have been proposed. Lacrimal scintigraphy is used to quantify the concentration of a radiopaque dye in tears and to indicate lacrimal passage (Garaszczuk et al., 2018). It is mainly used for research rather than for clinical purposes, as it is a complicated, expensive, time‐consuming and invasive procedure. The fluorescein tear clearance test was proposed to overcome these limitations. It assesses how quickly fluorescein dye disappears using a Schirmer strip (Garaszczuk et al., 2018). However, this method also has limitations, including the difficulty of comparing the concentrations of fluorescent dyes and subjective assessment. Therefore, ongoing research, even in human ophthalmology, continues to focus on developing a superior technique for measuring TCR. In other words, a simpler, more intuitive and quantifiable method is required for the early diagnosis of ocular surface diseases. Recently, a new method to easily measure the TCR using optical coherence tomography (OCT) has been developed (Zheng et al., 2014). In this technique, 5 μL of normal saline is dropped into the conjunctival sac, and the tear meniscus is photographed using anterior segment OCT (AS‐OCT). The method uses an OCT machine, recently introduced in ophthalmic hospitals, which can quantify the TCR; therefore, its use is expected to be widely adopted in clinical settings.

Several advances have been made in examining the pre‐existing tear layers, such as the development of the tear film break‐up time (TFBUT) test and STT. However, studies on the relationship between TCR and clinical symptoms are still lacking in the field of veterinary ophthalmology. Clinicians should consider the overall tear dynamics rather than each tear layer when diagnosing common and various ocular surface diseases. Furthermore, several attempts have been made in veterinary medicine to measure tear clearance (Dursun et al., 2002). The most recently introduced method involves assessing the TCR using a fluorescent dye, Schirmer strips and a filtered optical camera (Oriá et al., 2018). The area of the fluorescent material on the Schirmer strips from the conjunctival sac is photographed for approximately 1 h. However, these tests are not completely suitable due to difficulty in quantification, subjective results and the need for special equipment.

In this study, we investigated the potency of the recently developed method based on OCT to measure the TCR of dogs (Zheng et al., 2014) and explored the relationship between tear clearance and ocular symptoms.

sec.title: Animals
sec.p: Twenty client‐owned dogs that visited the ○○○○○○○ Veterinary Medical Teaching Hospital for ophthalmic examination were studied. As a routine procedure, the Veterinary Medical Teaching Hospital at ○○○○○○○ requested the owners of all animals enrolled in the study to fill out a patient consent form, which included a notification that patient information obtained during treatment may be used for research purposes. This study was conducted following all applicable regulations and guidelines, and all animals were treated in compliance with the ARVO Statement for the Use of Animals in Ophthalmic and Vision Research. All procedures in this study were approved by the ○○○○○○○ Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (approval no. ○○○○○). All dogs underwent complete ophthalmic examination, including slit‐lamp biomicroscopy (GS LED Slit Lamp MW50D, Shigiya Machinery Works LTD.), direct and indirect ophthalmoscopy and applanation tonometry (Tono‐Pen Vet; Reichert Technologies). Eyes without any clinical symptoms (such as discharge, hyperaemia, blepharospasm or rubbing) and ophthalmic diseases (such as glaucoma, uveitis and corneal ulcer) were included in this study.

sec.title: Ophthalmic examination and tear clearance measurement
sec.p: All procedures were performed without anaesthesia. The ophthalmic examinations and tear clearance measurements were performed under the control of the corresponding author, a board‐certified ophthalmologist. The blink rate of the left eye of each dog was recorded first for 5 min from a distance without eye touch. Then, the tear meniscus height (TMH) was photographed using AS‐OCT (RS‐3000 Advance OCT, NIDEK Inc.) with natural blinking (TMHbase) (Figure S1). The location was the median of the lower eyelid (Figure 1). The restraint method is the same as with slit bio‐microscopy. The dog was in a standing or sitting position, and its face and eyes were facing forward. Subsequently, the dog's front‐facing eyes faced the OCT's camera lens. The OCT machine accurately focused on the eye.

caption.p: Anterior segment optical coherence tomography (AS‐OCT) measurement of tear meniscus height (TMH).

sec.p: Next, as in human studies, 5 μL lukewarm normal saline was instilled into the lower conjunctival sac. It was confirmed that saline did not overflow from the tear meniscus and the TMH changed sufficiently to be measurable on the OCT (Zheng et al., 2014). The AS‐OCT images were captured immediately after instilling normal saline (TMH0) after 30 s (TMH0.5) and at 1 min intervals for 5 min (TMH1–5). The imaging using OCT and the calculation of TCR were carried out in the same way as in human studies (Zheng et al., 2014). The TCR was calculated using the following formula: TCR%=[TMH0−TMH0.5)/TMH0×100%.

Despite being confirmed that there was no significant difference between TMHbase and TMH5 (Figure 2), all dogs had about 1 h of resting time for accuracy before a routine complete ophthalmic examination, including STT‐1 and TFBUT tests. STT‐1 was measured for 1 min by inserting a Schirmer strip into the lower conjunctival sac, and TFBUT was measured as the time at which the fluorescein solution on corneal tear film began to dissociate and make a dry spot with the eyelids held open after eyelid blink.

caption.p: Mean tear meniscus height (TMH) over 5 min in the left eyes of dogs (n = 20). TMH was measured via anterior segment optical coherence tomography before saline instillation (TMHbase); immediately after instillation (TMH0); 30 s after instillation (TMH0.5); and 1–5 min after instillation (TMH1, TMH2, TMH3, TMH4 and TMH5, respectively). The graph represents the mean values with 95% confidence intervals. The difference among the mean TMH values over time was significant only for TMH0 (p < 0.05). The other TMH values were not significantly different (p > 0.05).

sec.p: As the software of the OCT machine did not support TMH measurement, image pixel size on the original image was measured using Adobe Photoshop (version 22.4.1, Adobe Inc.), which was converted to the actual length. A ratio of 2.04 was confirmed by comparing the measured pixel size and the actual length measured in the equipment. Therefore, 1 pixel was converted to 2.04 μm.

sec.title: Grouping
sec.p: To confirm the difference in terms of high or low TMHbase, we divided the patients into two groups (n = 10 in each group): High TMHbase included the patients with TMHbase values above the median (321.7 μm), whereas ‘Low TMHbase’ included those with TMHbase values <321.7 μm (Figure S1, Tables S1, S2).

Although only the patients without clinical symptoms were investigated, the STT‐1 and TFBUT scores of 11 patients were below the normal values (STT‐1 ≥ 15 mm/min and TFBUT ≥ 12 s) (Table S2). Therefore, these two main groups were subdivided into two subgroups: Satisfied and Not satisfied. The Satisfied subgroup included eyes with STT‐1 ≥ 15 mm/min and TFBUT ≥ 12 s, whereas the Not satisfied subgroup included those with STT‐1 < 15 mm/min or TFBUT < 12 s (Table S2). The STT‐1 score of 15 mm/min was based on the diagnostic criterion for quantitative Keratoconjunctivitis Sicca. However, the criteria for TFBUT vary greatly, and each study presented the average values for their study groups (Iwashita et al., 2023). Therefore, the TFBUT score of 12 s was based on the average value of the patients in this study (Iwashita et al., 2023).

sec.title: Data analysis
sec.p: The mean, median, standard deviation, minimum and maximum values were calculated for all data (age, blink rate, STT‐1, TFBUT and TMH). The correlation and differences among data were analysed using Spearman's correlation coefficient test. The differences in the TCR and blink rate of the Satisfied and Not satisfied groups were evaluated using the Wilcoxon signed‐rank test. Data were analysed using JMP version 10.0, a statistical software for Windows (SAS Japan Inc.). Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05.

sec.title: Signalment and values of ocular surface parameters
sec.p: A total of 20 left eyes were examined. The patients included seven Bichon Frises, six Miniature Poodles, two Malteses, two Mongrels, one Chihuahua, one Pomeranian and one Dachshund (Table S2). The mean (±standard deviation) blink rate, STT‐1 and TFBUT values were 59.3 ± 36.6 blinks/5 min, 19.9 ± 4.2 mm/min and 12.4 ± 7.2 s, respectively (Table 1).

caption.p: Mean and median values of each parameter.

fn.p: Abbreviations: BR, blink rate; SD, standard deviation; STT‐1, Schirmer tear test‐1; TCR, tear clearance rate (TCR (%) = [(TMH0 − TMH0.5)/TMH0]×100); TFBUT, tear film breakup time.

sec.title: TMH over time
sec.p: The TMH values at each time point were estimated for all obtained images. Some TMH values could not be obtained in five dogs because of the patient's poor cooperation and head shaking during the examination (Table S1). However, all TMH values could be obtained within 1 min after instillation without major problems. TMHbase and TMH1 values were obtained for all patients. Across the different time points, the mean TMH0 was significantly higher than the other TMH values (Figure 2). However, no significant difference was observed between the TMH0.5 and TMH5 values.

sec.title: Correlation between TCR and clinical data
sec.p: The TCR data and clinical data (breed, age, blink rate, STT‐1 and TFBUT) of the examined left eyes of all patients are shown in Tables S1 and S2, respectively. The mean TCR was 18.8% ± 14.0% (Table 1). TCR was negatively correlated with the blink rate (p = 0.02) but was not correlated with age, STT‐1 score or TFBUT (p > 0.05) (Table 2).

caption.p: Correlation between the tear clearance rate (TCR) and the variables of interest.

fn.p: Note: Data were analysed using Spearman's rank correlation (n = 20).
Abbreviations: BR, blink rate; STT‐1, Schirmer tear test‐1; TFBUT, tear film breakup time.

sec.title: Analysis by groups based on baseline tear meniscus height
sec.p: Next, we compared the TMH in eyes in the Not satisfied and Satisfied subgroups of the Low‐ and High‐TMHbase groups. The number of eyes in subgroups ‘High TMHbase – Not satisfied’, ‘High TMHbase – Satisfied’, ‘Low TMHbase – Not satisfied’ and ‘Low TMHbase – Satisfied’ was 8, 2, 3 and 7, respectively (Table 3). In the High TMHbase group, the median TCR of the Satisfied (22.49%) and Not satisfied (21.87%) subgroups was similar; however, in the Low TMHbase group, the median TCR of the Satisfied subgroup (14.69%) showed a significantly higher tendency than that of the Not satisfied subgroup (6.20%) (p = 0.02) (Figure 3). Similarly, in High TMHbase group, no significant difference was observed in median blink rate of the Satisfied (35 blinks/5 min) and Not satisfied (60 blinks/5 min) subgroups. In contrast, in the Low TMHbase group, the median blink rate of the Satisfied subgroup (40 blinks/5 min) was significantly lower than that of the Not Satisfied subgroup (105 blinks/5 min) (p = 0.04) (Figure 4).

caption.p: Analysis of the tear clearance rate (TCR) and blink rate by the group.

fn.p: Abbreviations: BR, blink rate (blinks/5 min);High, High TMHbase group; Low, Low TMHbase group; N, ‘Not satisfied’ subgroup, eyes with STT‐1 < 15 mm/min and TFBUT < 12 s; S, ‘Satisfied’ subgroup, eyes with STT‐1 ≥ 15 mm/min and TFBUT ≥ 12 s; SD, standard deviation; STT‐1, Schirmer tear test‐1; TCR (%) = [(TMH0 − TMH0.5)/TMH0]×100; TFBUT, tear film breakup time; TMHbase, tear meniscus height of the base.

caption.p: Median tear clearance rate values of the four groups. The patient eyes were divided into two groups: High tear meniscus height of the base (TMHbase) group (High, n = 10) and Low TMHbase (Low, n = 10). Eyes with Schirmer tear test‐1 (STT‐1) ≥ 15 mm/min and tear film break‐up time (TFBUT) ≥ 12 s formed the ‘Satisfied’ subgroup (S), and eyes that did not satisfy these conditions formed the ‘Not satisfied’ subgroup (N). The TMH values in the S and N subgroups in the High TMHbase group did not differ; however, a significant difference was confirmed between the S and N subgroups in the Low TMHbase group (p = 0.02), as determined using the Wilcoxon signed‐rank test. TCR, tear clearance rate.

Median blink rate of the four subgroups. The patient eyes were divided into two groups: High tear meniscus height of the base (TMHbase) group (High, n = 10) and Low TMHbase (Low, n = 10). Eyes with Schirmer tear test‐1 (STT‐1) ≥ 15 mm/min and TFBUT ≥ 12 s were considered the ‘Satisfied’ subgroup (S), whereas eyes that did not satisfy these conditions were termed the ‘Not satisfied’ subgroup (N). The blink rate was similar between the S and N subgroups in the High TMHbase group; however, a significant difference was confirmed between the S and N subgroups in the Low TMHbase group (p = 0.04) using the Wilcoxon signed‐rank test. BR, blink rate.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: This study found that TMH values in dog eyes were the highest immediately after saline instillation and returned to baseline values after 30 s. Therefore, it is reasonable to calculate the TCR using only two values, those at TMH0 and TMH0.5, as previously proposed for humans (Zheng et al., 2014). Therefore, this method is less susceptible to external factors, such as air humidity or wind, compared to those carried out over a longer duration. Additionally, unlike humans, dogs have difficulty cooperating with the procedure; therefore, measurements conducted over shorter durations could be more efficient and less stressful. The duration of 30 s was considered appropriate to ensure the stability of all 20 dogs during the scan. The non‐anaesthesia state is likely to be more closely related to tear‐related symptoms in everyday life than the anaesthetized state because the dog must naturally blink its eyes to clear tears. As there is no process of manually touching the eyes, manipulation of the glands or corneal–conjunctival irritation cannot occur.

The present findings confirmed a negative correlation between the TCR and blink rate. Ocular irritation symptoms are highly likely to occur in humans with low TCR (Sorbara et al., 2004). Dogs blink frequently when experiencing eye discomfort, which suggests that the blink rate may increase as a compensatory response to a low TCR (Butovich et al., 2011; Tsubota, 1998). We hypothesized that a low TCR leads to eye irritation, even in the absence of other ocular symptoms. Therefore, a low TCR and high blink rate indicate the need for close monitoring, as these patients may be more likely to develop ocular surface disease. According to a previous study, the average blink rate of dogs with no ocular disease symptoms was 11.5 ± 2.4 blinks/min, and brachycephalic dogs had a significantly higher blink rate than mesocephalic dogs (Williams & Denny, 2020), consistent with the already known fact that brachycephalic breeds are more likely to develop the ocular surface disease (Costa et al., 2021). Furthermore, some factors related to the skull shape have also been speculated to influence ocular surface discomfort (Faghihi & Rajaei, 2023). It is necessary to consider various factors, such as the cornea's curvature, eyelid laxity, the characteristic of tears, and the psychological condition, along with the breed and skull shape. However, to classify and consider all of these factors, especially given that external factors are unlimited, we focused on checking the feasibility of the test itself before conducting it. Therefore, the main significance of this study lies in the possibility of measuring TCR in dogs, and based on this, future research can be conducted for each breed or skull shape.

The findings revealed that TMH in all eyes was not significantly correlated with blink rate, STT‐1 and TFBUT. However, we obtained several useful results when comparing groups that were divided based on the results of TMHbase, STT‐1 and TFBUT. TCR and blink rates significantly differed between the Not Satisfied and Satisfied subgroups in the Low TMHbase group but not in the High TMHbase group. In the eye with relatively high TMH values, TCR and blink rates were less affected by low STT‐1 or TFBUT. These results suggest that eyes exhibiting a relatively low TMH but satisfying the criteria of the STT‐1 or TFBUT tests are more likely to remain free of ocular symptoms. On the contrary, a high TMH was not associated with any other clinical symptoms, even if some STT‐1 or TFBUT values were abnormal. This could be a possible reason for the large Not satisfied subgroup (n = 8/10) in the High TMHbase group, despite none of all eyes having any clinical ocular symptoms.

Veterinary ophthalmologists should be aware that a low TMH is a potential risk factor for ocular surface diseases. Based on this study, we formulated the following hypotheses: If tear clearance is inadequate due to the individual characteristics of the lacrimal functional unit, clinical homeostasis is maintained by blinking as a compensatory reaction. Disruption of homoeostasis and the inability to maintain a stable ocular surface can lead to ocular symptoms and diseases. Dogs with a low TMH are particularly sensitive in this regard, and various anatomical structures, such as corneal curvature, eyelid shape and orbital depth, influence TMH. Therefore, researchers should not only pay attention to the association between the TCR and ocular symptoms but also closely monitor a low TMH as an indirect risk factor for ocular surface diseases. In humans, a low TMH is also associated with dry eye disease, consistent with the results of this study (Czajkowski et al., 2012; Mainstone et al, 1996).

The TCR can measure the effect of multiple ocular components and not just the lacrimal functional unit (Zheng et al., 2016). Although all eyes in this study were normal, the TCR varied, which could be due to the variations in the ocular structures, such as the curvature of the cornea, length of the eyelid, shape of the inner canthus, size of the punctum and passage of the nasolacrimal duct. Analysing all possible mechanisms for each TCR value would require a highly comprehensive and complex process; however, studies have shown that low TCR has a marked negative effect on the health of the ocular surface in animals and humans (Zheng et al., 2016). In this study, low STT‐1 and/or TFBUT eyes showed low TCR in the low TMHbase group. Therefore, TCR has potential value in future ocular surface disease research and clinical applications.

This study has some limitations. Only a small number of eyes were examined; therefore, the results of this study need to be validated in future studies with larger sample sizes. However, to the best of our knowledge, to date, no study has reported the feasibility of examining the early changes in TMH after saline instillation using OCT in dogs. This study confirmed that TCR can be obtained even in dogs without special preparations, particularly without anaesthesia. Therefore, the present study can serve as a reference to conduct TCR analyses in a large population. Furthermore, developing software that can directly measure TMH on the machine will make measuring the TCR more convenient and accurate.

In conclusion, the TCR can be evaluated using AS‐OCT in dogs. This method can be a valuable diagnostic tool in veterinary ophthalmology. The measurement process takes less time, requires no special preparation and is simpler than other commonly used inspection methods. TMH merits further investigation to understand ocular surface health.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Project administration; writing‐original draft: Hyun‐Jeong Yoon. Formal and statistical analysis: Sangshin Park. Supervision; study design; manuscript review and editing: Joon‐Young Kim. All authors have read and approved the final manuscript.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors have no conflicts of interest to declare.

sec.title: FUNDING INFORMATION
sec.p: None.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors confirm that the ethical policies of the journal, as noted on the journal's author guidelines page, have been adhered to.

sec.title: PEER REVIEW
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://www.webofscience.com/api/gateway/wos/peer-review/10.1002/vms3.1334.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: No.

Oriá, A. P., Rebouças, M. F., Martins Filho, E., Dórea Neto, F. D. A., Raposo, A. C., & Sebbag, L. (2018). Photography‐based method for assessing fluorescein clearance test in dogs. BMC Veterinary Research, 14(1), 1–6. 10.1186/s12917-018-1593-y30176905PMC6122704

Sorbara, L., Simpson, T., Vaccari, S., Jones, L., & Fonn, D. (2004). Tear turnover rate is reduced in patients with symptomatic dry eye. Contact Lens and Anterior Eye, 27(1), 15–20. 10.1016/j.clae.2003.10.00116303522

Stern, M. E., Gao, J., Siemasko, K. F., Beuerman, R. W., & Pflugfelder, S. C. (2004). The role of the lacrimal functional unit in the pathophysiology of dry eye. Experimental Eye Research, 78(3), 409–416. 10.1016/j.exer.2003.09.00315106920
Tsubota, K. (1998). Tear dynamics and dry eye. Progress in Retinal and Eye Research, 17(4), 565–596. 10.1016/S1350-9462(98)00004-49777650
Williams, D., & Denny, E. (2020). Evaluation of blink rate in the normal dog. In BSAVA congress proceedings 2020 (pp. 461–461). BSAVA Library.

Zheng, X., Kamao, T., Yamaguchi, M., Sakane, Y., Goto, T., Inoue, Y., Shiraishi, A., & Ohashi, Y. (2014). New method for evaluation of early phase tear clearance by anterior segment optical coherence tomography. Acta Ophthalmologica, 92(2), e105–e111. 10.1111/aos.1226024020793

Zheng, X., Yamaguchi, M., Kamao, T., Sakane, Y., Goto, T., Shiraishi, A., & Ohashi, Y. (2016). Visualization of tear clearance using anterior segment optical coherence tomography and polymethylmethacrylate particles. Cornea, 35, S78–S82. 10.1097/ICO.000000000000099727617874
